Design a lean PRD template specifically for this take-home.

Requirements:
- Must capture all explicit requirements from the brief
- Must support vulnerable-customer considerations
- Must support FCA-regulated considerations
- Must remain concise
- Must avoid implementation details

- explain why it exists
- explain what information belongs there
- explain what should NOT be included

```
--- PAGE 1 ---
Ophelos — Engineering Take-
Home Task
The Ask
As part of your take-home Engineering Task, we would like you to attempt the
below problem statement.
Once you are happy with your solution, send over the solution along with the
supplementary work (see the Submission Checklist below) over to 
talent@ophelos.com. Please be sure to send this over at least 24h before your
interview. 
Customer Financial Health Feature
Role: Mid / Senior Software Engineer
Format: Any language or framework
About Ophelos
Ophelos is an AI-native debt resolution platform. We work with creditors to help
customers find their way out of debt fairly, and without the adversarial dynamic
that defines most of the collections industry.
We're authorised and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority. That means
the software we build has real obligations around data sensitivity, fair treatment of
vulnerable customers, and explainability of decisions. It's worth keeping that in
mind as you approach this task.
Our customers
People in debt, often in difficult financial situations. Many are financially
vulnerable. The product should treat them accordingly.
The Problem
Customers can already log their income and regular outgoings in our app.
That's where it stops. They record the numbers, but we give them nothing back,
no reflection of their financial position, no sense of whether their repayment
Ophelos — Engineering T ake-Home T ask
1
--- PAGE 2 ---
options are realistic, no way to track whether things are getting better or worse
over time.
We want to change that.
Build something that helps customers understand their financial health, and is
genuinely useful to someone trying to manage their way out of debt. This can
be any type of app e.g. CLI, REST API, Frontend-only, Small web app &amp; can use
any language or framework
This is intentionally open. You're not being given a spec, you're being given a
problem. What you choose to build, and how you frame the solution, is part of
what we're evaluating. Treat it as you would a real feature: scope it, make
decisions, and build what you think matters most.
Reference Data
This is an example of the income and expenditure data we currently collect. Use it
to understand the shape of what you're working with — it's not a schema
requirement.
Income Amount ␀£␀ Expenditure Amount ␀£␀
Salary 2,800 Mortgage 500
Other 300 Utilities 100
Travel 150
Food 500
Loan repayment 1,000
Your data model doesn't need to mirror this exactly.
Requirements
Must
Calculate and display a meaningful affordability assessment to the customer
Allow customers to track their financial position over time
Be well tested. Tests should protect something real, not just assert happy
paths
Should
Ophelos — Engineering T ake-Home T ask
2
--- PAGE 3 ---
Handle edge cases thoughtfully. What happens when income is zero, or
expenditure exceeds income?
Present information in a way that is appropriate for customers who may be in
financial difficulty
Reflect the FCA-regulated context. Consider how data is handled, what is
stored, and how decisions are surfaced to users
Stretch, only if all of the above is complete
Add currency and country_code fields with appropriate schema migrations
Implement secure statement sharing via a time-limited link
Generate a PDF export of the statement with appropriate branding
Stretch items must be well tested if attempted.
How We Want You to Work
Ophelos is an LLM-first engineering team. We expect — and actively want — you
to use AI coding tools throughout this task. Cursor, Claude, Copilot, whatever you
prefer. Using AI isn't a shortcut here; it's the expected way of working.
That said, we're as interested in how you use it as in what you produce.
Use an AI coding assistant. This is not optional.
Retain your full prompt history and include it in your submission. Export the
conversation from Cursor, share a Claude.ai chat link, or screenshot the
thread. We will look at this.
Write a DECISIONS.md in the root of your repo — spend 10␀15 minutes on it. What
did you choose to build? What did you leave out, and why? What would you
tackle next? This matters as much as the code.
Use any language or framework. If you pick something unfamiliar to
demonstrate AI-assisted ramp-up, that is actively welcome.
Don't over-engineer the submission. A working app with a clear README
beats a half-finished one with six abstraction layers.
Note how long you spent — we use this to calibrate difficulty over time.
What We're Looking For
Ophelos — Engineering T ake-Home T ask
3
--- PAGE 4 ---
We're not assessing whether you can implement a formula. We're looking for
evidence that you can think about a problem from a user's perspective, make
reasonable trade-offs, use AI tooling with judgment rather than on autopilot, and
write code that behaves correctly in the cases that actually matter.
The DECISIONS.md is your opportunity to show that thinking in writing.
Submission Checklist
Please be sure to send your submission over at least 24h before your interview. 
Code
GitHub repo (public or shared) with a README covering how to run it
Prompt history
Exported chat from your AI tool, or a shareable link to the conversation
DECISIONS.md
What you built, what you left out, what you'd do next, and why
Time spent
A rough note on how long you spent — doesn't need to be precise
Questions are welcome — if something is genuinely unclear, ask. If you're making
an assumption, note it in your DECISIONS.md rather than waiting for an answer.
Ophelos — Engineering T ake-Home T ask
4

```
</details>
</tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 I'm getting the full picture now. This is a take-home assignment for a mid to senior engineer role at Ophelos, an FCA-regulated debt resolution platform. The task is to build a customer financial health feature that gives users meaningful feedback on their income and expenses data—something they can already log but currently get no insights from. The scope is open, so I have flexibility with the tech stack and approach.

The core requirements are straightforward: calculate an affordability assessment, let customers track their financial position over time, and write meaningful tests covering edge cases like zero income or spending more than they earn. I need to be thoughtful about how I present this to people in financial difficulty and respect the FCA-regulated context around data handling and decision transparency. There are stretch goals around multi-currency support, secure statement sharing, and PDF exports, but those aren't essential.

The working style is clear—I should use AI tooling (expected, not optional), keep the full prompt history, document my decisions in a DECISIONS.md file, and avoid over-engineering. I need to submit a GitHub repo with a README, the prompt history, decisions document, and a note on time spent. Now I'm thinking about how to design a PRD template that fits this brief without being prescriptive, focusing on the core ask of affordability assessment and tracking rather than the more elaborate features I initially speculated about.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 I'm reviewing the SESSION_TEMPLATE to understand the naming conventions, and now I have a clearer picture of what the PRD template needs. The core requirements are straightforward: meaningful affordability assessment, tracking financial position over time, and solid testing. There are also some secondary considerations around edge cases that I should account for.

I'm noticing the existing /prd command already has a structure in place with sections like problem statement, users and jobs-to-be-done, goals, non-goals, requirements, success metrics, open questions, and traceability. I should mirror that structure but add take-home-specific framing, particularly around vulnerable customer considerations and FCA regulatory constraints. The template itself will be a practical fill-in form with guidance explaining why each section exists and what belongs in it.

I'm thinking about where to position the regulatory and vulnerability context — it could live as its own section between users and goals since it shapes the direction without being a requirement itself, or I could fold it into requirements with category tags. I'm also considering whether to capture the submission deliverables (DECISIONS.md, prompt history, time spent) as explicit requirements with a "Submission" category rather than as a separate section, since the brief explicitly lists them as part of what needs to be delivered. I should also check whether an "Assumptions" section would be useful given the brief invites them.

For the template itself, I'll structure it with the phase-gate header block, provide each section with guidance on what belongs and what doesn't, include placeholder text and example IDs, and make it clear this is the canonical structure the /prd command should follow. I'm planning to name it docs/PRD_TEMPLATE.md and include instructions on how to use it — replacing placeholders, maintaining append-only requirement IDs, keeping it concise, and avoiding implementation details. I'll add a framing block to each section that explains its purpose and scope, then provide a fill-in stub for the user to complete. This keeps the template concise while giving clear guidance on what belongs in each section and what to avoid.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

I have what I need. The brief is far more open than `TASK_ANALYSIS.md` speculated — the real MUSTs are just **affordability assessment, tracking over time, well-tested**, plus SHOULDs on edge cases / customer-appropriate presentation / FCA-regulated context, and stretch items on currency, secure sharing, PDF export. Plus meta-deliverables (DECISIONS.md, prompt history, time spent).

Now designing a lean template that fits the existing `/prd` command's spine (problem → users → goals → non-goals → requirements → success metrics → open questions → traceability) and adds the minimum extra structure the brief demands (a short regulatory/vulnerability stance, and submission deliverables tracked as requirements rather than a separate ad-hoc section).

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

Template written to `docs/PRD_TEMPLATE.md`. Key design choices:

**Spine kept aligned with `/prd` command and `phase-gate` skill** — same eight-section order plus one inserted section (Regulatory & duty-of-care context) so the PRD does not relitigate FCA / vulnerability inside every requirement row. Header block and traceability table follow `phase-gate` verbatim, so the template plugs in without diverging from the existing workflow.

**Brief coverage (auditable)** — every explicit MUST / SHOULD / Stretch / submission item from the PDF appears as a seeded row in Section 6 with a `Why` citation:

- MUSTs (`R1`–`R3`): affordability assessment, tracking over time, real tests
- SHOULDs (`R4`–`R6`): edge cases, customer-appropriate presentation, FCA context
- Stretch (`R7`–`R9`): currency/country, secure sharing, PDF export
- Submission (`R10`–`R13`): DECISIONS.md, prompt history, runnable repo + README, time spent

The submission items are treated as Must-priority requirements rather than a side channel — they're literally on the submission checklist, so they belong in the contract.

**Vulnerable customers shown twice, deliberately** — once as a *property of users* (Section 2 has a mandatory "vulnerability segments in scope" sub-list) and once as a *category of requirements* (`Vulnerability` tag in Section 6). The anti-patterns block at the bottom calls this out so future revisions can't silently collapse it to a single stretch row.

**FCA context as constraints, not features** — Section 3 caps it at 3–6 bullets, each pairing an obligation with a one-line product implication, and forbids inventing clause numbers (matches `.cursor/rules/10-evidence.mdc` anti-fabrication rule).

**Concise by construction** — explicit ~1–2 page target; bullets over paragraphs; a "delete these guidance blocks before committing" instruction; categories on requirement rows instead of separate sub-sections per concern.

**Implementation kept out** — every section's `Does NOT belong` line names the trap (no framework, no API shapes, no schema columns, no acceptance criteria). Section 6's note explicitly punts acceptance criteria to the test plan and forbids tech choices.

**Take-home-specific affordances**:
- Section 8 splits Open questions from Working assumptions (the brief invites assumptions over waiting), and the gate criteria require every question to have a paired default.
- Section 5 (Non-goals) is flagged as load-bearing since the brief is open-ended — this is where judgement is most visible to the reviewer.
- Submission deliverables sit inside Requirements so they get the same ID/traceability discipline as product features.

Not written: `docs/PRD.md` itself (per instructions). When `/prd` runs next, it should use this template as the form and pull content from `docs/discovery/NOTES.md` (which doesn't exist yet — `/discovery` is the prerequisite phase).
